text: The surgeon places ports medial to the left linea semilunaris and positions the camera at a 30-degree angle. The posterior defect is being closed, with visible larger and smaller gaps. The right posterior rectus sheath is seen on the far side of the screen. The surgeon sews the posterior sheath to the peritoneum using a 3-0 V-lock suture, carefully avoiding injury to the small bowel adjacent to the peritoneum. Fat tissue, likely a combination of peritoneum, omentum, and neoperitoneum, is visible and serves as a barrier between the mesh and intestine.

The surgeon carefully searches for smaller defects in the cephalad portion of the space, identifying a gap in the peritoneum medial to the patient's left posterior rectus sheath. The small bowel is visible beneath the peritoneum, necessitating caution to avoid injury during suturing. The surgeon considers options for closing larger defects, including harvesting the hernia pseudosac or using a patch material like Gorbile Aphasics or mesh. The decision is made not to perform a TAR, as the abdominal wall appears lax enough to close the defect without significant tension.

The surgeon uses an OV lock, one eighty, eighteen inch suture to close a hernia defect, employing a technique called progressive tensioning. Multiple sutures are placed initially without tightening, resembling a corset, to avoid breaking sutures and to manage the defect width. The pneumoperitoneum is lowered to about ten to facilitate reaching the high dome and grabbing the pseudosac. The ipsilateral linea alba is visible but harder to see, and care is taken to avoid losing it during closure.

The surgeon grabs a bite of the linea alba, including both the anterior rectus sheath and the cut posterior rectus sheath veil, which was cut about a centimeter away from the linea alba. The suture is pulled until resistance is felt, then the next suture is placed. The defect comes together without much resistance initially, but later requires progressive tensioning due to increased resistance. Small bites of the hernia pseudosac are taken to reduce seroma size. The surgeon parks the needle before placing the next suture to keep track of sutures. Progressive tensioning is applied, pulling the suture until resistance is felt, and using the same suture to continue suturing the linea alba and pseudosac. If the suture runs out, a new one is brought in. The final pull is towards the surgeon, with the left hand bracing the tissue to prevent cutting into the ipsilateral linea alba. The ipsilateral abdominal wall is expected to medialize, assisted by shifting the ports medially. Efficiency is emphasized by not dropping the needle and using two hands to manipulate sutures.

The surgeon adjusts hand positioning to improve suturing efficiency as they approach the cephalad portion of the defect closure. The left hand is repositioned to take advantage of a better angle for driving the needle, demonstrating the use of image inversion to facilitate this adjustment.

The surgeon performs image inversion, flipping the visual field upside down, allowing the left hand to function as the right hand and vice versa. This adjustment optimizes the angle for suturing, enhancing efficiency. The robotic system facilitates this process by allowing the surgeon to rotate the scope 180 degrees, changing the view from 30 degrees up to facing the floor. The surgeon reassigns the robotic arms at the console to accommodate the new orientation.

The bedside assistant swaps the instruments, resulting in arm four positioned between arms one and three, and arm two to the right of arm three. The image inversion technique is applied, flipping the orientation so the roof becomes the floor. The surgeon uses the needle driver in the right hand at an improved angle for efficient suturing. The surgeon continues to take bites of the linea alba, incorporating both the anterior rectus sheath and the posterior rectus sheath, avoiding the center due to the narrow diastasis.

The surgeon uses image inversion to efficiently suture a nine-centimeter defect, progressively tensioning the suture until resistance is felt. The defect closes easily without requiring a unilateral or bilateral tar, despite the needle driver occasionally popping off the suture.

The surgeon closes a 14-centimeter defect in the abdominal wall without performing a transversus abdominis release (TAR). The defect edges are compressed together using both hands, and the suture line is run back by grabbing the veil of the posterior rectus sheath. The surgeon performs additional stitches to lock the suture as the suture length runs out, and then reverts the image back to right side up to complete the procedure.

With the image inverted, the surgeon smoothly measures the floor of the defect to prepare for mesh placement. The mesh is intended to cover the entire floor, and its dimensions are measured to be approximately thirty-two centimeters long, with an additional two centimeters for potential stretching.

The surgeon places a Pry-Teen mesh measuring 30 by 45 centimeters into the space through an 8 millimeter port, ensuring it tucks under the diaphragm for good overlap. The mesh appears slightly redundant due to a mis-measurement, folding on itself within the abdominal cavity.
